## [00:00 - 03:00]

Okay, so I walk through how to identify potential joint venture partners and how to build value in their lives, how to build relationships. Now I just want to talk about actually contacting them. So one of the things you really want to avoid is just sending a blind email to someone or sending a blind social media like a Facebook message or something to someone if they don't know who you are. First you want to build that relationship or at least put yourself on their radar, you know, through the ways I talked about in the last video. But then the actual, how do you approach them? The best way to do this is at a live event. Really, I love live events. I think they are amazing for building your business for a lot of reasons, but one of them is the networking with partners. There is no better way to meet someone than to like buy them a beer, you know, or buy them lunch. It's the absolute best way to meet them and get to know them. If you can't get to a live event or they're not a live event and you can't meet them that way, then probably the next best way is the phone. And some people, you know, it can vary by the market, vary by the contact. A lot of people don't love to get on the phone, but the phone can be good or direct mail like real physical mail. But better than direct mail is a FedEx. If you send a FedEx package to someone, they're going to open that up. And beyond that, then you get down into the realm of social media or email. So absolute best way to do is live event, meeting them face to face, go out to the bar with them, go out to lunch with them, go out to dinner with them. Beyond that, you probably want to do either phone or FedEx or, you know, just a regular old standard direct mail. And the last place in terms of preferred contacts would be like social media or email. Now depending on the market, you might actually need to educate them on what a joint venture partner is. You know, there are some markets like, you know, John Gallagher who's in that edible medicinal plants and herbs market that foraging, going out in your backyard and foraging for food or foraging for medicine in your backyard. In that market, he has had to educate people on what joint ventures are and how they work. Ruth Buzinski, another one of my Platinum members, she trains psychiatrists and psychologists how to treat their patients better and then how to build their businesses. And in that market, there aren't a lot of JVs going back and forth. So she's really had to work on educating people. You know, in my market where I'm teaching people how to build their business online, everyone knows all about JVs and the joint ventures are happening all the time. So I don't do any education. But if you're in the, you know, cake decorating market or something like that, then you very much might have to go out, build those relationships and educate them on what joint ventures are and how you can, you know, help your, the people on your list, help your clients, help

## [03:00 - 06:03]

your prospects and make more money if you promote for each other. Then once you make contact and you're actually going to, you know, you've built value for them and maybe you've promoted their product, when it's time to ask them to promote your product, there's certain things that people are going to be looking for when they're looking to promote your product. First of all, they're going to want to know whether they can make some money, whether you have a process that will actually convert their leads. You can show them your launch. I mean, that will put you head and shoulders above everyone else. Show them the metrics on your launch. By metrics, I mean, how much money did your internal launch make on a per person on your list, you know, based on the people on your list, based on how many visits you sent to your pre-launch content, how many, you know, unique visitors did you send to your pre-launch content, what were your results per opt-in, what were your results? Show them the dollars per visitor, the dollars per opt-in. And you know, the thing is, is when you go to them, you can't, you can say, well, this is my internal launch. I can't guarantee that your traffic will convert as well, but this is a tested offer. It's a tested sales process, and these are my numbers. It's a tested launch. You know, I did this launch, I did an internal launch. It generated this many, these kind of dollars per subscriber. And when you go to someone, you can share those kind of metrics, then that will give them a lot more confidence in your process. So they're going to be looking for a process that converts. They're going to be looking for that you've got a great product. So you want to show them your product, you want to show them testimonials, you want to show them the results that your clients got. Because if they're going to promote for you, they're sort of, they're dependent on how you treat, their relationship is dependent on how you treat the people they send to you. So you want to show them that you're going to take care of the people that come and opt in your list, you're going to take care of them. You're going to give them good pre-launch content, and then you're going to make an offer for a great product. And if they buy the product, they're going to get great results. So they're going to care about the quality of the product, the quality of the conversion. They're going to care that you actually pay them for the product. Now this might sound amazing, but it happens all the time where there are people that, you promote for someone else and you never actually get paid. I've actually done this. It doesn't happen all the time, but it absolutely happens. So they're going to want to know and have the confidence that you are going to get paid. Basically, this conversation is all about what's in it for them. What is in it for them? Why they can trust you with their sending their traffic to you. Why they can trust you with sending their list to your people. They want to know that they're going to convert, that it's a great product, that you're not a slime ball, that you're going to track properly, and then they're eventually going to get paid. All very, very important considerations. And one other thing they're going to be interested in, they might be interested in, are you going

## [06:03 - 06:47]

to be able to promote for them later on, or maybe promote for them first. So there's a lot of reciprocity where one person promotes for the other person and then they turn around and promote back. That happens all the time. So those are some of the things that they are going to be looking for when they are trying to decide whether they want to promote for you. Another big one is, does the offer match their list? If they have a list about golf and you come to them and you want to sell stuff about how to be a locksmith, it's not going to be a good match. They're not going to want to mail. They want a good match. They want their list to match up to your pre-lunch content and to your product. So keep all those things in mind when you go to talk to them, when you make that first contact to see if they want to promote for you.
